html:
Best Linking Group Holdings (HKEX:9882) is no longer mainly a clean slewing-ring manufacturer story. The live filings show a stranger transition: a small, tightly controlled industrial supplier is trying to become a Southeast Asia construction and mining procurement platform, with slewing rings, replacement parts, excavators, generators, and nickel ore moving through the same customer network.

That matters because the market can be wrong in two opposite ways. On stale trailing numbers, the stock looks expensive: HK$1.27 per share, about HK$1.02 billion market cap, 800 million shares, and roughly HK$983 million EV per Fintel. Against 2025 revenue of HK$155.4 million and a HK$2.8 million loss in the 2025 annual results, this is not statistically cheap. But on a forward transition view, the stock is a tight-float option on whether 2025’s ugly mix shift becomes a higher-revenue platform with recovered gross-profit dollars.

The prior renewable/wind bearing thesis should be discarded. Best Linking’s actual 2025 revenue mix was slewing rings HK$18.5 million, mechanical parts HK$22.8 million, machineries HK$73.9 million, and nickel ore HK$40.1 million. The high-quality part of the business is smaller than hoped, but not dead: slewing rings were only 11.9% of revenue yet 28.4% of gross profit, while mechanical parts were 14.7% of revenue and 31.8% of gross profit. The low-margin machinery and mineral lines are the volume wedge.

The variant perception is that the market may be seeing “margin collapse” when the more useful question is whether Best Linking is buying distribution scale. The Philippines became the largest market at HK$64.7 million, up 181.6% year over year and 41.6% of total revenue. China was only 7.9% of revenue. That is a very different setup from a tiny supplier trapped in the Chinese property downturn. Management attributes the Philippines growth to construction and mining activity returning to normal, with demand for machinery and consumable replacement parts. If Best Linking can attach higher-margin parts and slewing rings to these machinery and mining customers, the 2025 low-margin revenue base becomes customer acquisition cost rather than evidence of permanent degradation.

The second point is the Japan OEM call option. Slewing ring OEM revenue was tiny, but it grew from HK$0.4 million to HK$2.1 million, and the company incorporated a Japanese subsidiary to develop OEM business in Japan. For a microcap with 2025 operating expenses of roughly HK$21 million, even HK$20 million to HK$30 million of incremental high-spec OEM or replacement-parts revenue can move earnings meaningfully. The old factory economics are still attractive: depreciation is already in the cost base, and slewing rings and parts carry gross margins that appear near 30%, versus 7.9% for machineries and about mid-single-digit economics for minerals.

The third point is current-cycle commodity support. Best Linking is not a nickel producer, so it should not get mining-company multiples. Still, nickel ore trading has become material, and nickel recently traded around US$19,000 per tonne, up sharply year over year according to Trading Economics. Stronger nickel and Southeast Asian mine activity help volumes and customer relevance, even if the margin remains a trading spread.

The balance sheet keeps the thesis alive. At year-end 2025, cash was HK$42.1 million, bank borrowings were HK$9.4 million, current ratio was 7.6x, and there were no material contingent liabilities. Net assets were HK$135.8 million, or only HK$0.17 per share, so asset value does not protect the current price. But the company is not financially distressed. The real risk is not solvency, it is that revenue scales through low-margin trading while operating cash flow stays negative. The 2025 annual report showed operating cash outflow of HK$13.7 million and capex of HK$2.2 million, so the next validation point is gross-profit dollars and cash conversion, not headline revenue.

The retail context is important. The stock has already moved from the HK$0.50 to HK$0.60 area earlier in 2026 to around HK$1.27, with Chinese broker/news feeds showing sharp single-day moves around the results and annual-report window. This is a 75% controlled, small-float stock, with bank covenants tied to Mr. Chan Yuk Pan remaining the key shareholder. That structure cuts both ways: upside can overshoot fundamentals if the revenue growth narrative catches, but the downside air pocket is real if the market decides 2025 was just low-quality trading growth. The annual general meeting mandate to issue up to 20% of shares, noted in recent company-announcement coverage, is standard in Hong Kong but still a dilution risk.

My base thesis is not “this is cheap today.” It is: Best Linking has a narrow but real path to making today’s valuation look acceptable if 2026 and 2027 revenue doubles again, the Philippines/mining customer base sticks, and higher-margin rings and mechanical parts recover as a share of gross profit. A plausible 2027 case is HK$320 million to HK$380 million of revenue, 16% to 18% gross margin, HK$25 million to HK$35 million of net income, and positive operating cash flow. On HK$0.03 to HK$0.04 EPS, the stock can hold a high multiple because the float is tight and the growth screen would look unusual for a neglected HK microcap.

Bear case: HK$0.55, down 57%. Revenue growth remains mostly machineries and nickel ore, gross margin stays near 13%, operating cash flow remains negative, and the market values the company closer to 3x book or 2x to 3x sales.

Base case: HK$1.45, up 14%. Revenue compounds to HK$300 million plus, gross margin recovers toward 16%, Japan/PRC OEM continues from a small base, and the stock sustains a premium transition multiple despite limited current earnings.

Bull case: HK$2.60, up 105%. Best Linking becomes a recognized Southeast Asia mining and construction procurement platform, revenue reaches HK$500 million plus, gross profit exceeds HK$80 million, OEM and replacement-parts attach rates rise, and the tight float re-rates the stock to a high-growth microcap multiple.

Current stock price: HK$1.27. The upside torque is real, but it is forward-execution torque rather than present-day value.
